Show 7 excluded companies
  • 9101 NYK Line (日本郵船) — R- (FY3/2026 revenue -6.4%, OP -34.3%; container rate decline)
  • 9107 K-Line (川崎汽船) — R- (FY3/2026 revenue -2.8%, OP -18.2%, NP -56.5%; container oversupply + Middle East fuel costs)
  • 9110 NS United Kaiun (NSユナイテッド海運) — R- (FY3/2026 revenue ¥207B -16.3%, OP -25.3%; NP +29.4% on vessel sale gains. Yen appreciation headwind)
  • 9115 Meiji Kaiun / Meikai Group (明治海運/明海グループ) — R- (FY3/2026 recurring profit -86.9% to ¥1.19B; revenue prior period -9.28%)
  • 9119 Iino Kaiun (飯野海運) — R- on earnings (revenue -10.3%, OP -21.4%) — but stock UP on Hormuz reopening hopes (売上×株価○, outside R+ scope)
  • 9179 Kawasaki Kinkai Kisen (川崎近海汽船) — Data limited — FY3/2026 specific results not retrieved. K-Line coastal subsidiary; assumed marine downcycle R-. Flagged for review.
  • 9308 Inui Kisen (乾汽船) — R- (FY3/2026 OP -77.8% to ¥810M, recurring -84.5%; dividend slashed ¥76 → ¥6.79. H1 revenue -6.0%)

Universe coverage is exhaustive for the 10 currently-listed TOPIX-33 Marine names. 9179 Kawasaki Kinkai Kisen data limited and flagged.

For marine FY3/2026 — container rate fall caused all majors' operating profit to decline. The active default is 売上○ (only MOL qualifies). Would normally default to 営業利益○ when container rates recover.

Why these filter options?

Why use Revenue ↑ (default for Marine this year)?
In plain terms: For commodity-cycle sectors we normally default to 営業利益○ (the oil sector approach) — but for marine FY3/2026 EVERY major company had operating profit decline because container shipping rates fell sharply from 2024 peaks. Under 営業利益○ filter, the marine sector would show 0 companies — not useful.



So for marine this year, 売上○ is the active default. Only MOL (商船三井) qualifies — revenue +2.0% (Q3 YTD) thanks to LNG/chemical tanker stability, but operating profit -57.1% due to ONE container-shipping affiliate's profit collapse. This is the classic 'revenue up, profit compressed' pattern.



The other 3 majors are excluded: NYK Line (revenue -6.4%, OP -34.3%) — R-. K-Line (revenue -2.8%, OP -18.2%) — R-. Iino Kaiun (revenue -10.3%, OP -21.4%) — R- (despite stock being up on Hormuz reopening hopes — 売上×株価○ pattern).



Next year, when container rates eventually recover and operating profit comes back, 営業利益○ default would likely return for marine. The filter framework reads each year's data and adapts.
Why use Operating profit ↑ (would normally be default — but shows 0 this year)?
In plain terms: For commodity-cycle sectors this is normally the right default (see the oil sector explanation). For marine FY3/2026, however, every major company saw operating profit decline because container shipping rates collapsed. So this filter shows 0 companies this year. Switch back to 売上○ to see the only qualifying name (MOL).



Keep this filter option available — when container rates recover in future years, this will likely become the active default for marine again, matching the oil sector pattern.
Why use Either ↑ (same as 売上○ for marine this year)?
In plain terms: This is the union of revenue-up and operating-profit-up filters. For marine this year, since 営業利益○ shows 0 companies, this filter shows the same set as 売上○ (just MOL).
Non-qualifying marine majors (R-) — transparency note

The following marine sector majors are R- (revenue down) under all filter modes this year, hence excluded from the R+ cards above. Listed here for transparency:

  • 9101: NYK Line — FY3/2026 revenue -6.4% to ¥2.42T, OP -34.3% to ¥139B. Container rate decline + auto cost rise. R- under all filters. High dividend (¥230 → ¥200 FY27 plan) is the income story.
  • 9107: K-Line — FY3/2026 revenue -2.8% to ¥1.02T, OP -18.2% to ¥84B, NP -56.5%. Container oversupply + Middle East fuel cost. R- under all filters. Dividend ¥120 maintained.
  • 9110: NS United Kaiun — FY3/2026 revenue ¥207B (-16.3%), OP ¥15.1B (-25.3%); NP +29.4% on old vessel sale gains. R- on revenue + OP. Yen appreciation headwind.
  • 9115: Meiji Kaiun (Meikai Group) — FY3/2026 recurring profit ¥1.19B (-86.9%, beat ¥600M guidance). Revenue down -9.28% prior period. R- on both. FY3/2027 guidance recurring ¥5B (4.2x).
  • 9119: Iino Kaiun — FY3/2026 revenue -10.3% to ¥127B, OP -21.4%. R- on earnings BUT stock significantly up on Hormuz reopening expectations + real estate uplift — 売上×株価○ pattern (outside R+ scope).
  • 9179: Kawasaki Kinkai Kisen — FY3/2026 specific data not surfaced in this analysis. Coastal/domestic shipping subsidiary of K-Line. Data limited — flagged for review; assumed marine downcycle R-.
  • 9308: Inui Kisen — FY3/2026 revenue ¥32.3B (+1.8% guidance, but H1 actuals -6.0%), OP -77.8% to ¥810M, recurring -84.5%. Dividend slashed ¥76 → ¥6.79. External market deteriorated. R- on OP collapse.
▸ International shipping — revenue resilient, profit compressed 2 names
Both companies operate in international ocean shipping (diversified/tanker) and share the dominant FY3/2026 pattern: revenue held up or grew modestly while operating profit fell sharply, driven by rate-cycle declines and sustained cost inflation. Both trade at sub-1x PBR and lean on long-term contract segments as a buffer.
9104 Mitsui O.S.K. Lines (MOL) ▸
Revenue: ↑ +2.8% Op profit: ↓ -58.1% Net profit: ↓ -58% Stock: flat / range-bound
分類: 総合海運大手 / Major diversified shipping
Genuine research (combined-prompt verification) 📚 Original source_hint: Combined-prompt JP web search 2026-06 + kabutan 9104 + minkabu 9104 + MOL IR Q2 2025 + EBC Financi…
Company overview: Mitsui O.S.K. Lines (MOL, 9104) is one of Japan's three major shipping companies, with diversified operations across container shipping (via 3-company ONE equity stake), LNG carriers, chemical tankers, car carriers, dry bulk, plus real estate and passenger ships — a 'comprehensive shipping group.' Its hallmark is balance: 'high-amplitude container shipping' alongside 'stable LNG / car carrier long-term contracts.' Put simply: MOL is a 'trading conglomerate of ocean logistics' — an infrastructure player supporting global supply chains. How did business move? FY3/2026 full-year actuals: Revenue ¥1.825 trillion (+2.8%), recurring profit ¥175.8B (-58.1%), NP also -58% (source: Nikkei 2025-11-04). Classic profit-compression pattern. Nov 2025 mid-year revision cut recurring guidance ¥170B → ¥152B (actual landed at ¥175.8B). Dividend lifted from initial ¥175 to ¥200 (+¥25); FY3/2027 guidance ¥205 (sources: Nikkei TDnet, Yahoo Finance 9104 dividend). (Note: prior version's claim of ¥230 → ¥200 dividend cut was incorrect — actual is raise from ¥175 to ¥200.) PBR ~0.79x, payout ratio 32.3%, div yield ~3.6%. Why did business move this way? The 'sales-up / profit-down' pattern has three clear drivers. 1. Sharp container-rate decline — 2024 saw abnormally high container rates due to Red Sea rerouting demand, but 2025-2026 saw a sharp reversal. ONE (Ocean Network Express, 3-company JV equity-method affiliate) profit contribution collapsed — the single largest factor behind the -57.1% ordinary profit drop. 2. Long-term contracts in LNG / chemical tankers / car carriers cushion the floor — These are mid-long term charter contracts with fixed rates, insulated from cycles unlike container shipping, keeping overall revenue slightly positive. 3. Sustained-high fuel + labor costs — Heavy-fuel-oil prices stayed elevated, crew wages rose; cost increases compounded with rate declines, double-pressuring margin. Why did the stock move this way? The stock lacks clear directional momentum — range-bound. Three reasons why the stock isn't falling sharply despite halved profit. 1. 'Cheap value + high dividend' positioning — PBR ~0.67x (Mar 2025) deep discount + high dividend yield; institutional investors hold MOL as 'cyclical but paid to wait' value. Profit halving is already priced in; further downside is limited. 2. Management's PBR-improvement commitment — Mid-term plan explicitly targets 'steady ¥200-300B annual profit + sustained dividends + PBR re-rating from 0.67x to 1.0x → 1.2-1.5x.' Management's intent supports the floor. 3. LNG business re-rated on decarbonization theme — LNG carrier business is positioned as 'bridge-energy' transport in the decarbonization transition, expected to grow mid-long term as a growth engine filling the container-cycle trough.
9130 Kyoei Tanker (共栄タンカー) ▸
Revenue: ↑ up Op profit: ↓ down Net profit: ↓ down Stock:
分類: タンカー専業 / Specialty tanker (VLCC/VLGC)
Genuine research (combined-prompt verification) 📚 Original source_hint: Combined-prompt JP web search 2026-06 + minkabu 9130 settlement + Yahoo Finance 9130 + kabuyoho 91…
Company overview: Kyoei Tanker (9130) is a Tokyo-Chiyoda-headquartered ocean-shipping specialist founded 1954, focused on crude oil and liquefied-gas transport via VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier, ~300,000 dwt) and VLGC (Very Large LPG Carrier) vessels — a mid-tier specialty tanker company. It is a Nippon Yusen (NYK) Group company — NYK is the principal shareholder with a 30.01% stake (sources: irbank E04251, kitaishihon 9130). Main customers include Nippon Yusen and Cosmo Oil; operations combine long-term charters + spot transport. Fleet of ~10-15 vessels; mid-tier player ranked 3rd-4th domestically. Put simply: 'a Nippon Yusen group specialty tanker company moving energy from around the world.' How did business move? FY3/2026 actual: Revenue ¥15.5B (up YoY), NP ¥414M (sharp decline) (source: minkabu 9130/settlement). Beat original revenue guidance (¥14.5B / -4.4%) but landed in profit decline. Dividend ¥40 (¥20+¥20), unchanged. Stock ~¥1,331 (Jun 2026), PBR 0.72x, ROE 23.01% (sources: Yahoo Finance 9130, irbank, kabuyoho). Why did business move this way? Three reasons per company disclosure. 1. VLCC volatile but firm — China crude-stockpiling demand at start, winter weakness, then WS70s spike from Trump administration's reinforced ship-sanctions (source: company disclosure). 2. VLGC strong — emerging-market civilian-fuel demand + Panama Canal restrictions + Red Sea-driven Cape routing extending ton-mile (source: company disclosure). 3. Cost stack — VLCC1 large-scale repair downtime + costs, vessel-sale revenue loss, inflation-driven ship costs, Mexico port-denial special loss, sustained operating costs + rate hikes (source: company disclosure). Why did the stock move this way? Stock at ¥1,331 (Jun 2026), -2.28% one day (source: Yahoo Finance). No specific analyst/news commentary on stock direction found in sources — verifiable structural anchors only. 1. ROE 23.01% — high profitability even in soft-earnings periods (source: irbank/kabuyoho). 2. PBR 0.72x — cheap on book value (source: kabuyoho). 3. NYK Group affiliation with long-term-charter-led structure: NYK 30.01% principal shareholder, Cosmo Oil among main customers, long-term contracts dominate (sources: irbank E04251, kitaishihon 9130). Specific institutional-flow attribution is UNVERIFIED in public sources.
▸ Domestic coastal shipping — modal-shift tailwind & re-rating 1 names
Kuribayashi Shosen operates domestic coastal RoRo routes (Hokkaido–Honshu), largely insulated from international rate cycles. Its story is driven by government-mandated modal-shift policy (truck-to-ship), stable cargo demand, and a stock re-rating triggered by capital-efficiency improvements via policy-stock sales.
9171 Kuribayashi Shosen (栗林商船) ▸
Revenue: ↑ +1% Op profit: ↓ -7% Net profit: ↑ +88% Stock: +26% (limit-up on revision day 2025-09-22; full YoY not stated)
分類: 内航RoRo海運 / Coastal RoRo shipping
Genuine research (combined-prompt verification) 📚 Original source_hint: Combined-prompt JP web search 2026-06 + minkabu 9171 + Nikkei 9171 limit-up article + kabutan 9171…
Company overview: Kuribayashi Shosen (9171) is a Tokyo (Chiyoda-Otemachi) headquartered coastal-shipping company — founded 1894 (Meiji 27) and incorporated 1919 (Taisho 8). The core business is coastal large RoRo (Roll-on/Roll-off, vehicles drive themselves onto the ship) cargo shipping, operating routes connecting Hokkaido (Tomakomai / Kushiro) - Sendai - Tokyo - Shimizu - Nagoya - Osaka. The traditional core cargo has been newspaper paper and other paper-related products, with Oji Logistics named as a main customer. Beyond that, the company carries chassis cargo (steel, building materials), commercial vehicles (autos), and miscellaneous freight, supporting Hokkaido-Honshu logistics. Consolidated segments include shipping plus hotel operations in Hokkaido (Noboribetsu Grand Hotel etc.) and real estate operations (centered on Muroran). In short: a long-established coastal-shipping group whose core is large RoRo between Hokkaido and Honshu, with additional Hokkaido hotel and real-estate businesses. How did business move? FY3/2026 full-year forecast (post-upward-revision): Revenue ¥53.7B (+1%), OP ¥2.5B (-7%), recurring ¥2.8B, NP ¥3.7B (+88%). The NP surge came from selling 3 policy-held stocks in Oct-Dec 2025 and booking a ¥1.9B special gain, a one-off. Why did business move this way? The earnings structure decomposes into 3 reasons. 1. Revenue +1% sustained = modal shift + stable Hokkaido-Honshu logistics demand — The 2024 Problem (driver labor-hour rules) is government-driving modal shift from truck to ship, supporting firm coastal RoRo cargo demand. 2. OP -7% due to fuel + labor + delayed rate revisions — Sustained heavy-fuel-oil prices + industry-wide seafarer shortage driving wage hikes structurally lifted costs, while coastal rate revisions require negotiation with shippers and lag the cost increase. 3. NP +88% is a one-off from special gain — In Oct-Dec 2025, Kuribayashi sold 3 policy-held stocks and booked a ¥1.9B special gain (UNVERIFIED: whether SMFG / Tokio Marine HD were specifically among the three issuers sold could not be independently confirmed in this verification), upward-revising full-year NP to ¥3.7B (+88%). Why did the stock move this way? Stock hit limit-up at ¥1,440 (+¥300, +26%) on the upward-revision announcement day (2025-09-22). Three main reasons. 1. Policy-stock sale = expectation of capital-efficiency improvement — TSE PBR-improvement push makes rationalizing policy holdings (equity stakes held purely for business-relationship maintenance) well-rated by institutions as capital-cost-conscious management. Expectations rose for further policy-stock sales + stronger shareholder returns. 2. Stable Hokkaido-Honshu coastal RoRo position re-rated — Coastal RoRo requires specialized port facilities + vessels + operating know-how, making new entry difficult. Kuribayashi Hokkaido-Honshu network supports the view that mid-term rate revisions will recover today cost pressure. 3. Modal-shift policy structural tailwind — MLIT logistics-efficiency policy (truck to rail / ship) is a structural tailwind for coastal shipping, prompting investors to re-rate Kuribayashi mid-term growth story.
TOPIX-33 sector: 空運業 (Air Transportation)
Total listed in sector 4
Qualifying as R+ 4
R- (currently listed) 0
Delisted / TOB (historical) 0
Math: 4 R+ + 0 R- = 4 currently listed. 0 delisted/TOB shown separately.
For air transportation, revenue-up captures business quality — passenger volume × fare + cargo. FY3/2026 all 4 listed carriers were R+; FY3/2027 profit guidance weak across the sector on fuel-cost shock.

Why these filter options?

Why use Revenue ↑ (default)?
In plain terms: For air transportation, revenue is a clean signal of business quality. Airline revenue moves with passenger volume × average fare (plus cargo). When revenue grows, the business is genuinely doing more — more seats sold, higher fares, or more cargo carried. Fuel cost (which can squeeze profit) is a cost-side issue, not a revenue-side one.



For FY3/2026 specifically, every Japanese listed airline grew revenue — the sector was uniformly R+. JAL +9.1%, ANA +12.3%, Skymark +1.4%, Star Flyer +4.4%. All four benefited from inbound tourism (weak yen), US business travel surge (Trump tariffs), and air cargo strength. However, forward FY3/2027 profit guidance is universally weak across the sector because Middle East tensions (Iran-related) are pushing jet fuel costs sharply higher — fuel is ~25% of an airline's cost base, so when oil prices rise, profits get squeezed even if revenue keeps growing.



That's why this sector defaults to 売上○ — revenue is the truer signal of business health than profit, which is being distorted by the fuel-cost shock right now.
▸ Major carriers: record year, fuel-cost cliff ahead 2 names
Both JAL and ANA delivered record or all-time-high FY2026 profits on the back of inbound tourism, trans-Pacific business travel, and an air cargo boom, only to guide for roughly 20–43% profit declines in FY2027 due to surging jet fuel costs tied to Middle East tensions. Their stocks rose to 52-week highs on the strong results, then sold off sharply when the cautious forward guidance and analyst downgrades arrived.
9201 Japan Airlines ▸
Revenue: ↑ +9.1% Op profit: ↑ +26.4% Net profit: ↑ +28.6% Stock: -20% from 52-wk high (¥3,272→¥2,671)
分類: フルサービス大手 / Full-service major (international + cargo)
Genuine research (combined-prompt verification) 📚 Verification audit trail: [1] FY2026 Revenue ¥2,012.5B (+9.1%) confirmed by AeroTime + Traicy + JAL press release. [2] F…
Company overview: Japan Airlines (JAL, TSE: 9201) is one of Japan's two dominant full-service carriers. It flies domestic Japanese routes and international routes across Asia, North America, and Europe. Under its umbrella it also runs two low-cost subsidiaries: ZIPAIR (long-haul budget) and Spring Japan (short-haul budget). Revenue comes mainly from international passenger fares, with domestic passengers, air cargo, and maintenance services also contributing. JAL went bankrupt in 2010 and relisted on the Tokyo Stock Exchange in 2012 — it has been steadily rebuilding since. How did business move? For the fiscal year ending March 2026 (FY2026), JAL delivered its best results since relisting. Revenue: ¥2,012.5 billion (~$13.4B), up 9.1% year-on-year — a new post-relisting record. Operating profit (EBIT): ¥218.0 billion (~$1.45B), up 26.4% — profits grew about three times faster than revenue, which is a sign of strong operating leverage. Net profit: ¥137.6 billion, up 28.6%. EBIT margin: 10.8%, beating JAL's own medium-term target of 10%. Annual dividend raised to ¥96/share (up ¥10), a sign of management confidence. For next year (FY2027, ending March 2027), JAL's own guidance is markedly more cautious: revenue is expected to grow slightly to ¥2,095 billion (+4%), but EBIT is forecast to drop ~18% to ¥180 billion and net profit ~20% to ¥110 billion. A record profit year followed by a 20%-lower forecast is what drove the recent stock sell-off. Why did business move this way? 1. Inbound tourism surge: A weak yen made Japan very affordable for foreign visitors, and they came in large numbers. JAL's international passenger count rose 5.6% and international passenger revenue rose 9.1%. Occupancy above 85% also meant JAL could push fares higher — when seats are nearly full, airlines have more pricing power. 2. US business travel spike driven by tariffs: Trump-era tariffs on Japan prompted Japanese companies to negotiate in person with US partners and customers, driving a surge in outbound business-class bookings on trans-Pacific routes. Business class fares are several times economy fares, so even a modest shift in mix toward business travelers meaningfully lifts revenue per flight. 3. Air cargo boom: International cargo revenue jumped 21.3%. E-commerce growth and companies restructuring supply chains both drove demand for fast air freight. 4. FY2027 profit drop guided — fuel cost is the main culprit: Middle East tensions (Iran-related conflict) pushed oil prices higher, raising jet fuel costs. Jet fuel is roughly 25% of JAL's total cost base, so when oil prices rise, the impact on profit is direct and large. Management also cited uncertainty in global demand as a reason to be conservative. Why did the stock move this way? 1. Record results drove the stock to a 52-week high (~¥3,272): Consecutive quarters of strong results, powered by inbound tourism and cargo, lifted the stock steadily from around ¥2,082 (March 2024) to a high of about ¥3,272. Sustained good news over multiple quarters tends to steadily raise a stock price as investors become more optimistic. 2. FY2027 profit guidance caused a sharp sell-off (late April 2026): Announcing a ~20% profit drop for the coming year immediately after a record year was a surprise to the market. Investors who had priced in continued growth sold quickly when that growth story paused — the stock dropped from the high area toward ¥2,406 (52-week low). The logic: if next year looks worse, today's price was too high. 3. Multiple analyst downgrades amplified the decline (April–May 2026): Nomura cut JAL from Buy to Neutral, slashing its target from ¥3,700 to ¥2,600 (April 15). Morgan Stanley MUFG cut from Overweight to Equal-weight, with target from ¥3,700 to ¥2,500 (April 28). Several other brokers trimmed targets too. Downgrades are not a 'sell' signal but a 'we're less bullish' signal — when multiple big banks move simultaneously, institutional investors tend to reduce positions, adding selling pressure. 4. Ongoing fuel-cost and oil-price fears kept a lid on recovery: Each news item about Middle East tensions or JAL raising fuel surcharges reminded the market that the FY2027 profit concern was real, preventing a sharp bounce. Current price (early June 2026): approximately ¥2,640–2,671. About 20% below the 52-week high of ¥3,272, but recovered from the 52-week low of ¥2,406. The analyst consensus target is ~¥3,038, implying roughly 14% upside — suggesting the market may have oversold, but there is no strong near-term catalyst to close that gap quickly.
9202 ANA Holdings ▸
Revenue: ↑ +12.3% Op profit: ↑ +10.6% Net profit: ↑ +10.5% Stock: -17% from 52-wk high (¥3,350→¥2,717)
分類: フルサービス大手 / Full-service major (international + cargo + LCC)
Genuine research (combined-prompt verification) 📚 Verification audit trail: [1] FY2026 Revenue ¥2,539.2B (+12.3%) confirmed by ANA IR + BigGo Finance TDnet + AeroMorning.…
Company overview: ANA Holdings (Tokyo Stock Exchange: 9202) is Japan's largest airline group. Its core business is ANA (All Nippon Airways), a full-service carrier operating domestic and international passenger routes. It also owns Peach Aviation (a budget airline), AirJapan (a mid-priced Asia-focused brand), and since August 2025, Nippon Cargo Airlines (NCA), Japan's only dedicated freight carrier. Think of it as a three-tier structure: premium full-service airline, budget airline, and cargo — all under one holding company. How did business move? For FY2026 (April 2025 – March 2026), ANA Holdings posted record-high results across the board: operating revenue of ¥2.539 trillion (+12.3% year-on-year — a very large number for a Japanese airline), operating profit of ¥217.4 billion (+10.6%), and net profit of ¥169.0 billion (+10.5%). An operating margin of around 8.6% is healthy for a post-COVID airline. The annual dividend was raised to ¥65 per share (up ¥5 from the prior year — a sign of confidence). However, for FY2027 (ending March 2027), the company's own guidance is much grimmer: operating profit ¥150 billion (-31%), net profit ¥96 billion (-43%). Revenue is still expected to grow to ¥2.77 trillion (+9.1%), but profits are forecast to collapse — which is a significant red flag investors noticed immediately. Why did business move this way? The record profit in FY2026 was driven by three main forces. First, surging inbound tourism: a weak yen made Japan cheap for foreign visitors, filling international flights and pushing up average fares. New routes from Haneda to Milan, Stockholm, and Istanbul achieved roughly 80% load factors — an impressive fill rate that shows strong demand. Second, the acquisition of NCA (Nippon Cargo Airlines), completed August 2025 after multiple regulatory delays, added NCA's revenues into the consolidated numbers for the second half of the year. Third, financial recovery from COVID was now fully reflected — the equity ratio improved to 37.7%, meaning the balance sheet is in much better shape than during the pandemic years. The big profit drop expected in FY2027 is almost entirely due to jet fuel costs surging because of Middle East tensions. Jet fuel is one of airlines' biggest expenses, and when the price jumps sharply, profits take a direct hit. ANA is budgeting for Singapore kerosene at $200/barrel in Q1 of FY2027 — extremely expensive compared to recent norms. The company plans to offset about ¥60 billion of the estimated ¥140 billion fuel cost increase through fuel surcharges, hedging, and cost cuts, but cannot absorb it all. A separate structural drag: business travel on domestic routes has not fully recovered post-COVID, as remote work became permanent for many corporate employees. Why did the stock move this way? Over the past year the stock followed a clear arc: up sharply in autumn 2025, then down after the April 2026 earnings. The 52-week range is roughly ¥2,510 – ¥3,350, and as of late May/early June 2026 the stock trades around ¥2,700–¥2,800 — down 15–20% from its peak. What drove the stock UP (autumn 2025): Two catalysts hit close together. In October 2025, ANA raised its full-year earnings forecast (an upward revision signals the business is beating expectations — markets reward that). Then in November 2025, ANA announced a massive shareholder return package: up to ¥150 billion in share buybacks (14% of shares outstanding — a very large buyback) plus a ¥200 billion hybrid bond issuance to fund aircraft investment. Buybacks directly increase earnings per share by shrinking the share count, which investors treat as a clear positive. The stock climbed to the ¥3,300–¥3,400 range. What drove the stock DOWN (spring 2026): At the April 2026 earnings release, the record FY2026 results were good — but the FY2027 guidance (net profit -43%) shocked the market. Investors look forward, not backward, so a -43% profit warning hit hard even though the current year was excellent. Multiple analysts, including JPMorgan, cut their price targets in response (e.g., JPMorgan reduced from ¥3,300 to ¥3,200 while keeping a Neutral rating). When many analysts lower targets at once, institutional investors often trim positions, adding selling pressure on the stock.
▸ Mid-tier carriers: squeezed margins, structural pressure 2 names
Skymark and Star Flyer both grew revenue but saw net profit fall materially — Skymark trapped between major-carrier price wars and LCC competition that eroded load factors despite fare hikes, and Star Flyer hit by large yen-weakness forex losses on dollar-denominated aircraft leases that wiped out operating gains at the bottom line. Both carry thin operating margins with little buffer against cost shocks, and both have guided for further profit compression in FY2027.
9204 Skymark Airlines ▸
Revenue: ↑ +1.4% Op profit: ↓ -1.4% Net profit: ↓ -24% Stock: -23% from 52-wk high (¥530→¥406)
分類: 中堅国内航空 / Mid-tier domestic carrier
Genuine research (combined-prompt verification) 📚 Stock code 9204 confirmed correct for Skymark Airlines on TSE Growth Market. Verification audit: [1] FY2026 Revenue ¥110…
Company overview: Skymark Airlines (TSE: 9204, Tokyo Stock Exchange Growth Market) is Japan's third-largest airline. It operates scheduled domestic routes out of Tokyo Haneda to major cities including Fukuoka, Sapporo, and Okinawa. It sits in an awkward middle ground — not a full-service carrier like JAL or ANA, but not an ultra-low-cost carrier like Peach or Jetstar either. The company went bankrupt in 2015 and was delisted, but was rebuilt with backing from private equity firm Integral and ANA Holdings (still a shareholder) and relisted on the TSE Growth Market in December 2022. How did business move? Revenue: ¥110.4 billion (~$760M USD), up 1.4% year-on-year — a record high on paper. But the way it got there matters: the airline raised prices and accepted fewer passengers (down 1.8% to roughly 8 million). Revenue up, seats filled less. Operating profit: ¥1.8 billion (~$12M USD), down 1.4%. On ¥110 billion in revenue, earning only ¥1.8 billion means an operating margin of just 1.6% — razor thin. This is normal for budget airlines under stress, but not comforting. Ordinary profit: ¥2.9 billion, up 282%. This sounds great but is misleading — it was boosted by a foreign exchange accounting gain (the yen was weak, so overseas assets converted to more yen at year-end). That gain is one-off and is not in next year's forecast. Net profit: ¥1.64 billion, down 24% from the prior year. The bottom line shrank even as revenue hit a record. Dividend: ¥7 per share at year-end — more than double the prior year's ¥3. A positive gesture for shareholders, even though profits fell. FY2027 guidance (year ending March 2027): Revenue ¥120.8 billion (+9%), operating profit ¥1.5 billion (down further), net profit ¥800 million (roughly half FY2026). The company expects to grow its top line significantly — partly from the new 737 MAX fleet and route expansion — but expects profits to keep falling as those costs ramp up. Grow now, profit later is the implicit message. Why did business move this way? 1. Price war with the big carriers: JAL and ANA ran aggressive time-limited sales that cut fares and pulled passengers away from Skymark. When a major carrier dropped prices 5%, its load factor jumped nearly 10 percentage points. Skymark did the opposite — it raised fares 7.5% — and its load factor fell over 6 points on key routes like Haneda–Fukuoka. Because Skymark only flies domestic, there is no international cushion. Skymark actually petitioned regulators to consider guidelines limiting below-cost chronic discounting — a sign of how badly the competition was hurting it. 2. Costs rising faster than fares: Fuel costs (crude oil priced in dollars, paid when the yen is weak), crew wages (industry-wide labor shortage driving raises), airport fee hikes, and transition costs for the new 737 MAX fleet all piled up at once. Since 2018, Skymark raised average fares 12% but operating costs jumped 32%. The gap keeps widening. 3. Foreign exchange working both ways: A weak yen makes dollar-denominated fuel and maintenance more expensive during the year. But at year-end, dollar assets convert to more yen, creating a paper gain. This gave FY2026 results a flattering one-off boost that will not repeat. 4. Stuck in the middle: Without the brand loyalty of JAL/ANA or the ultra-low cost base of LCCs, Skymark finds it hard to defend market share. Business travelers gravitate to full-service; price-sensitive leisure travelers go to LCCs. That structural squeeze is the underlying theme across every quarterly report. Upcoming structural fix: Skymark received Japan's first Boeing 737 MAX 8 in early May 2026 and launched commercial service on Haneda–Fukuoka on May 28, 2026. The new aircraft burns about 14–15% less fuel than the old 737-800s. This is the key reason management guides to higher revenue while accepting still-lower near-term profit — the fleet transition costs money upfront but should improve margins once the fleet is established. International routes are also planned for around 2030. Why did the stock move this way? The stock was around ¥1,272 at its IPO (December 2022), briefly hit ¥1,545, and has fallen roughly 68% since then to around ¥406 as of June 5, 2026. The 52-week range alone is ¥318–¥530. Key catalysts: 1. February 2025 profit warning — the biggest single catalyst: Skymark slashed its FY2025 operating profit forecast by 87%, from ¥3.5 billion down to just ¥600 million. The stated reasons were intensified competition from major carriers' time sales and yen-related cost increases. The stock fell more than 10% in a single day. For investors, a near-90% cut to the profit forecast in the same fiscal year is a serious confidence breaker — it suggested management had poor visibility into its own competitive environment. 2. Prolonged low profitability: FY2025 and FY2026 both came in with operating profit below ¥2 billion, and the FY2027 guidance is even lower at ¥1.5 billion. For a company with ¥110 billion in revenue, that margin leaves almost no room for error. Investors price thin-margin businesses at a discount because any cost shock can tip them into a loss, so they don't pay much for earnings that could evaporate quickly. 3. The re-listing narrative failed to deliver: At the 2022 IPO, Skymark was framed as a post-COVID recovery story with a leaner cost structure after its bankruptcy restructuring. That story relied on the competitive environment remaining manageable — it didn't. The gap between IPO expectations and actual results drove a sustained re-rating lower. 4. Recent partial recovery (May 2026): After the FY2026 results were published in mid-May 2026, the stock bounced from a recent low around ¥318 to around ¥433 — roughly a 36% move in days. The first 737 MAX delivery, the doubled dividend, and a broker upgrade to bullish with a ¥520 target all contributed. However, a separate broker lowered its target to ¥340 in the same period, reflecting the split view on whether the fleet renewal fixes the structural problem or just defers it.
9206 Star Flyer ▸
Revenue: ↑ +4.4% Op profit: ↑ +12.9% Net profit: ↓ -77.4% Stock: -23% from 52-wk high (¥2,481→¥1,903)
分類: プレミアム・リージョナル / Premium regional
Genuine research (combined-prompt verification) 📚 Verification audit trail: [1] FY2026 Revenue ¥44.8B (+4.4%) confirmed by irbank + data-max + Nikkei article — three-way …
Company overview: Star Flyer (SFJ, code 9206) is a small regional airline headquartered in Kitakyushu, Fukuoka Prefecture. Founded in 2002 and listed on the Tokyo Stock Exchange Standard Market in 2011, the company operates 11 Airbus A320-family aircraft on five domestic routes linking Kitakyushu, Fukuoka, Osaka/Kansai, Yamaguchi-Ube, and Naha with Tokyo Haneda. ANA Holdings is the largest shareholder at around 14%, and all routes are code-shared with ANA. What sets Star Flyer apart is a deliberate premium-regional positioning: every seat has black leather upholstery with generous legroom — a noticeable step above LCCs and even some legacy carrier products. Annual revenue is around ¥45 billion (roughly $300 million), making it a small but well-known niche carrier with about 700 employees. How did business move? The latest full-year results (FY March 2026) show operating revenue of ¥44.8 billion (+4.4% year-on-year — a new revenue high), and operating profit of ¥1.39 billion (+12.9% — decent progress for a company of this size). Load factor hit 81.6% and paying passengers reached 1.654 million (+3.9%), both healthy demand signals. However, the headline that hurt was the bottom line: ordinary profit crashed 64.6% to ¥684 million, and net profit dropped 77.4% to just ¥434 million. The reason was a ¥526 million foreign-exchange loss (explained below). No dividend was paid. For FY March 2027 (current year), the company guides revenue of ¥47.9 billion (+6.8%), but operating profit of only ¥660 million — a 52.5% fall from the year before, which is a steep drop that spooked investors. Why did business move this way? Three forces drove the changes: (1) Passenger demand recovery. After COVID wiped out ¥11.2 billion in operating profit in FY2021 alone, the airline clawed back passengers year by year. By FY2026, load factor was at an all-time high since listing — essentially, full planes again. This drove steady revenue and operating profit growth. (2) Yen weakness creating forex losses. Star Flyer's aircraft are on finance leases denominated in US dollars. When the yen depreciates, the outstanding dollar-denominated debt becomes worth more in yen terms, and that gain in liability value is recorded as a forex loss. In FY2026, yen weakness was worse than the company's hedging assumed, generating a ¥526 million hit below the operating profit line — nearly erasing all the operating gains at the bottom line. (3) Rising maintenance cost provisions. In the first half of FY2026 (April–September 2025), the company recorded higher provisions for scheduled aircraft maintenance paid in foreign currency — again worsened by yen weakness — plus higher staff costs. This pushed the first-half ordinary profit into a ¥370 million deficit, triggering a major guidance downgrade mid-year. The FY2027 operating profit decline is driven by rising fuel costs (crude oil) and ongoing yen weakness increasing operating expenses. Why did the stock move this way? The stock traded between a 52-week high of roughly ¥2,481 and a low of ¥1,900, sitting near the low (~¥1,903) as of early June 2026 — a decline of around 23% from the peak. (1) International route restart announcement (positive catalyst, ~September 2025): Star Flyer announced it would resume Kitakyushu–Taipei scheduled service in autumn 2026 — its first international flights in 6.5 years. The stock jumped ~50 yen (+2.1%) on the day. Why: international routes carry higher fares than domestic, so investors saw this as a revenue growth story that domestic-only operations couldn't offer. (2) First-half earnings collapse and guidance cut (negative, October 2025): When the H1 FY2026 results landed on October 31, 2025, ordinary profit had swung from a ¥640 million profit to a ¥370 million loss versus the same period a year earlier. The company slashed its full-year guidance. Why: the market had expected a gradual improvement; instead it got a sharp reversal caused by forex and maintenance cost spikes, which are harder to control than passenger demand. (3) Off-hours block sale (negative, May 2025): An existing shareholder sold 440,000 shares (~5% of all shares) in a single off-market transaction. Why: when a large block of shares suddenly hits the market, supply jumps, prices typically fall. The stock dropped sharply from announcement day through pricing. (4) FY2026 full-year results + steep FY2027 guidance (negative, late April 2026): Net profit down 77% for the year, and guidance for the next year showing operating profit halved. Why: investors price stocks on future earnings expectations; when guidance shows earnings getting worse, not better, they sell. (5) Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism stern warning (negative, May 19, 2026): The regulator formally warned Star Flyer for failing to update maintenance procedures for infant life jackets (a 2022 Airbus spec change was never reflected) and for deliberately concealing the error for nearly a year. Why: safety compliance issues at airlines can affect passenger trust, raise the risk of further regulatory action, and signal management control weaknesses — all negatives for the stock.

No companies qualifying as R+×S+ under the current filter for this sector this year.

TOPIX-33 sector: 水産・農林業 (Fishery, Agriculture & Forestry)
Total listed in sector 12
Qualifying as R+ 8
R- (currently listed) 0
Delisted / TOB (historical) 0
For Fishery / Agriculture / Forestry, revenue-up captures real business growth — volume × price for each segment. All 8 covered companies were R+ in the latest FY.

Why these filter options?

Why use Revenue ↑ (default)?
In plain terms: For Fishery, Agriculture & Forestry, revenue is a clean signal of business growth. Seafood revenue moves with volume × price; seed revenue moves with units sold × ARPU; food-producer revenue moves with output × per-product price. None of these are distorted by raw commodity prices the way oil revenue is. So when revenue grows, the business is genuinely doing more — more seafood sold, more seeds shipped, more chicken processed.



For FY-latest, all 8 covered companies are R+: Nissui (+5.1%), Maruha Nichiro (+4.7%), Kyokuyo (+10.5%), Sakata Seed (+4.8%), Kaneko Seeds (+3.1% guidance), Hokuto (+1.9%), Akikawa Bokuen (+4.1%), Axyz (+2.3%). Stock performance varies sharply — Nissui +55%, Axyz +57%, Maruha Nichiro +22%; Kyokuyo -21%, Hokuto -2%, others ~flat — which is exactly why the bucket categorization matters: each cluster has its own underlying story.
▸ Seafood mid-cap — revenue up on price, profit & stock squeezed 1 names
Kyokuyo's revenue grew double-digits because seafood market prices rose globally, but volumes declined as consumers cut back on more expensive fish. Operating profit fell and the company cut full-year guidance, sending the stock down 21% from its February 2026 peak. A classic 'revenue up, profit & stock down' pattern in a commodity-sensitive sector.
1301 Kyokuyo ▸
Revenue: ↑ +10.5% Op profit: ↓ -3.1% Net profit: ↑ +1.5% Stock: High ¥5,450 (Feb 2026) → current ~¥4,285 (Jun 2026); approx -21% from 52-week high
分類: 水産大手 / Major seafood company
Genuine research (combined-prompt verification) 📚 Revenue ¥334.6B / OP ¥10.7B / NP ¥6.84B confirmed Yahoo Finance JP + Minato Shimbun. Dividend ¥150 confirmed multiple so…
Company overview: Kyokuyo (founded 1937, TSE Prime) is one of Japan's major seafood companies. Its core business — accounting for roughly 60% of sales — involves sourcing, processing, and distributing a wide range of seafood (tuna, salmon, shrimp, cod roe, crab, etc.) from global markets to Japanese buyers. Three other segments round out the business: a Fresh Food segment selling chilled fish at supermarkets, a Food Products segment making frozen prepared dishes (simmered fish, grilled fish, seafood snacks), and a Logistics Services segment handling cold-chain distribution. The company also participates in tuna aquaculture and land-based salmon farming to secure stable supply. How did business move? The most recent completed fiscal year, FY2026 (April 2025 – March 2026), showed revenue of ¥334.6 billion (+10.5% year-on-year — a healthy jump), but operating profit fell to ¥10.7 billion (-3.1%) and ordinary profit dropped to ¥10.0 billion (-7.6%). Net profit was ¥6.84 billion (+1.5%), roughly flat. In other words, the company sold more but earned less per yen of sales. The dividend rose from ¥130 to ¥150 per share — a positive signal. For FY2027, management is guiding for revenue of ¥365 billion and operating profit of ¥12.0 billion, implying a meaningful recovery. Why did business move this way? Revenue grew because seafood market prices surged globally — salmon, crab, and cod roe all became more expensive, inflating the top line. But higher prices caused consumers to buy less by volume, so salmon and shrimp unit sales fell. In the frozen food segment, rising raw material costs forced Kyokuyo to raise prices on everyday items like simmered and grilled fish — and convenience-store buyers cut orders in response. The company had set itself ambitious profit targets at the start of FY2026 and was forced to cut those targets in February 2026 (ordinary profit guidance slashed from ¥12.5B to ¥10.3B). Why did the stock move this way? Over the past year the stock has swung sharply. It hit a low of around ¥3,615 in April 2025 because the Trump administration's reciprocal tariff announcement triggered a broad selloff across Japanese equities. From there it rebounded strongly, reaching a 52-week high of ¥5,450 on February 27, 2026, driven by FY2025's strong results and a Nikkei article in October 2024 spotlighting large-scale shipments from what is billed as the world's largest land-based Atlantic salmon farm in Mie Prefecture. After that peak, the February 2026 earnings downgrade and renewed tariff concerns pushed the stock back to the low ¥4,000s. As of June 5, 2026 it trades around ¥4,285 — a P/E of roughly 7x, well below market.
▸ Seed & agricultural-input recovery 1 names
Kaneko Seeds and Sakata Seed both posted record operating profits as agricultural materials demand normalized post-COVID, premium-variety mix improved margins, and (for Sakata) overseas vegetable seed sales benefited from climate change driving farmer demand for heat-resistant and disease-resistant varieties. Both have low PBR; one (Sakata) executed a self-tender offer to absorb a major shareholder's sale.
1376 Kaneko Seeds ▸
Revenue: ↑ +3.1% (FY2026 guidance) Op profit: ↑ +25.7% (FY2026 guidance) Net profit: ↑ +25.0% (FY2026 guidance) Stock: Approx -13% from Oct 2025 peak (¥1,628 → ¥1,417 Jun 2026); broadly flat to modest decline YoY
分類: Agricultural Seeds & Inputs / 農業種苗・農材
Genuine research (combined-prompt verification) 📚 Code 1376 confirmed as Kaneko Seeds. All P&L in ¥M (irbank initial fetch had unit-scale error — corrected). FY2025 r…
Company overview: Kaneko Seeds (founded 1895, TSE Standard) is an agricultural conglomerate headquartered in Maebashi, Gunma. It operates four segments: Seeds (vegetable/flower/forage crop seed breeding and sales), Floriculture (flower seedlings, bulbs, garden products), Agricultural Materials (pesticides, coated fertilizers, herbicides), and Facility Materials (greenhouse systems, hydroponics, agri-structures). Agricultural Materials is the largest segment (>50% of revenue), and the biggest profit driver. ~695 employees, revenue ~¥64–66 billion. How did business move? FY2025 (year ended May 2025): revenue ¥64.5B (+4.7%), operating profit ¥1,512M (+2.2%), ordinary profit ¥1,666M (+6.1%), net profit ¥1,200M (+1.9%). For FY2026, 9-month YTD through Feb 2026 showed revenue ¥42.1B (+2.1%), operating profit ¥475M (+29.1%), net profit ¥370M (+29.8%). Unchanged full-year guidance: revenue ¥66.5B (+3.1%), operating profit ¥1,900M (+25.7% — multi-year high target), net profit ¥1,500M (+25%), dividend ¥48. Why did business move this way? Recovery in FY2025–FY2026 driven by: (1) Agricultural Materials rebound — because demand for herbicides and rice-paddy pesticides recovered and the product mix shifted to higher-margin items. (2) Facility Materials profit recovery — because the FY2024 material-cost surge had suppressed farm-equipment investment; as pressure eased, greenhouse and hydroponics orders returned strongly (+80% segment profit). (3) Seeds segment margin compression — because rising labor costs and expanded logistics infrastructure added fixed costs. (4) Floriculture weakness — because the COVID-era gardening boom faded. Why did the stock move this way? Stock traded ¥1,310–¥1,628 over the past year, peaking in October 2025, currently around ¥1,417 (early June 2026). (1) October 2025 surge — because Q1 FY2026 net profit came in at 2.8× the prior year, a strong positive surprise. (2) Post-peak drift lower — because despite solid progress, management left full-year guidance unchanged (no upward revision). (3) Low valuation floor — because the stock trades at just 0.61× book value, attracting value-oriented buyers on dips. (4) No institutional analyst coverage — zero broker ratings means retail-driven trading and amplified swings on earnings.
▸ Domestic food-shortage beneficiaries 1 names
Hokuto (mushrooms) and Axyz (poultry) both benefited from supply shortfalls elsewhere in the food chain that pushed domestic prices higher. For Hokuto, 2024 vegetable shortages drove shoppers toward stable factory-grown mushrooms; for Axyz, avian flu disruptions abroad raised domestic chicken prices while feed costs fell. Both saw sharp profit jumps, though Hokuto's market viewed FY2025 as one-off while Axyz's stock rose 57% on durable margin expansion.
1379 Hokuto ▸
Revenue: ↑ +1.9% Op profit: ↓ ~-12% (FY26 ~¥58B vs FY25 ¥66B) Net profit: ↓ ~-2% Stock: Roughly flat to modest decline; 52-week range ¥1,690–¥2,123, current ~¥1,840–¥1,900
分類: きのこ生産・販売 / Mushroom Production & Sales
Genuine research (combined-prompt verification) 📚 FY2025 actuals (Rev ¥83.1B / OP ¥6.63B / NP ¥4.44B) confirmed across IRBank, Moneyworld/QUICK, Logmi Finance, Nikkei dis…
Company overview: Hokuto is Japan's largest mushroom producer, headquartered in Nagano. It grows Bunashimeji (beech mushrooms), Eringi (king oyster), and Maitake at scale in controlled indoor factories, selling to supermarkets and food manufacturers nationwide. Also has operations in North America, Taiwan, and Malaysia, plus smaller processed-food and chemical-products (mushroom cultivation materials) businesses. TSE Prime; market cap ~¥60 billion. How did business move? FY2025 (ended March 2025) confirmed actuals: revenue ¥83.1B (+4.6%), operating profit ¥6.63B (+108% — nearly doubled), net profit ¥4.44B (+26%). FY2026 (ended March 2026): the company initially guided for OP -35% on price normalization, but after an upward revision in November 2025, final results were revenue ¥84.7B (+1.9%, third consecutive record), net profit ~¥43B (-2%). Dividend raised from ¥50 to ¥55. Why did business move this way? (1) Mushroom prices rose because vegetables got expensive — 2024 summer heat waves and rain caused cabbage/lettuce supply shortfalls, lifting all supermarket vegetable prices. Shoppers shifted to mushrooms as a stable, factory-grown alternative. (2) A factory fire in October 2024 hurt less than feared — a Bunashimeji plant in Ueda, Nagano burned (~7% of national output), but other plants absorbed the shortfall and insurance proceeds offset the loss. (3) Overseas wins and a weak yen helped — North American sales grew, and currency translation boosted reported earnings. (4) Acquisition of Funagata Mushroom (April 2026) added button-mushroom capacity. Why did the stock move this way? 52-week range ¥1,690–¥2,123, settling in the ¥1,800–¥1,900 band. (1) November 2025 upward earnings revision lifted the stock — Q2 beat estimates and the full-year forecast was raised, drawing in buyers. (2) Dividend increase (¥50 → ¥55) attracted income investors. (3) Stock upside was capped because FY2025's profit spike was seen as a one-off tied to unusual vegetable prices unlikely to persist. (4) The Funagata Mushroom acquisition added modest growth optionality.
▸ Organic-subscription micro-cap recovery (with caveats) 1 names
Akikawa Bokuen swung back to operating profit (¥-3M loss → ¥+143M profit) and ordinary profit rose 261% as price hikes finally flowed through and Chinese subsidiary contribution added scale. However, net profit fell 25% due to a ¥143M impairment write-down on direct-sales and Chinese subsidiary assets, and FY2027 guidance points to profit declining again. Thinly traded ¥4.2B market-cap stock with no analyst coverage.
1380 Akikawa Bokuen ▸
Revenue: ↑ +4.1% Op profit: ↑ turnaround (prior ▲¥3M → +¥143M) Net profit: ↓ -25% (impairment charge ¥143M) Stock: Roughly flat YoY (~¥990–1,005 now vs ~¥1,009 a year ago; 52W range ¥982–¥1,094, intra-year peak ~¥1,250)
分類: 有機農畜産・産直宅配 / Organic Farm-to-Home Subscription
Genuine research (combined-prompt verification) 📚 FY2026 actuals (Rev ¥8,283M / OP ¥143M / Ordinary ¥186M / NP ¥21M) confirmed across IRBank + Yahoo Finance + Strainer + …
Company overview: Akikawa Bokuen (Yamaguchi Prefecture, est. 1972) is a vertically integrated organic farm-to-home food company. It raises antibiotic-free chickens and pesticide-free vegetables on its own farms, then ships them directly to subscriber households — about 2,000 product lines including chicken, pork, beef, eggs, dairy, vegetables, and frozen prepared foods. Think of it as a premium organic subscription that actually owns and runs the farms. TSE Standard; market cap ~¥4.2 billion (micro-cap). Two segments: Production & Wholesale (~81%) and Direct Sales (~19%). How did business move? FY2026 (ended March 2026), announced May 15 2026: Revenue ¥8,283M (+4.1% YoY — 4th straight year of top-line growth), Operating Profit ¥143M (vs. a ¥3M operating loss in FY2025 — clean swing back to black), Ordinary Profit ¥186M (+261% YoY, 55% above the company's own ¥120M forecast — a meaningful beat), Net Income ¥21M (-25% YoY). Net income fell because the company booked ¥143M in impairment losses on direct-sales and Chinese subsidiary assets. Below the operating line the business is clearly recovering; at net income the write-down muddied the picture. Dividend held flat at ¥10. Why did business move this way? Three years of consecutive profit declines had three causes: (1) feed costs surged because Japan imports most animal feed grain — yen weakness drove input costs higher; (2) a new direct-sales logistics center opened in November 2023 added heavy depreciation before revenue benefits materialized; (3) post-COVID hangover as lockdown subscribers cancelled. FY2026 recovery: (1) frozen processed foods grew; (2) multiple price hikes finally flowed through; (3) the Chinese subsidiary was consolidated in March 2024, adding revenue. The impairment reflects honest write-down of underperforming assets. Why did the stock move this way? 52-week range ~¥982–¥1,094, current ~¥990–1,005, broadly flat YoY. Intra-year peak ~¥1,250. Stock rose toward the peak because quarterly earnings tracked well ahead of company targets — by Q3 the company had hit 103% of full-year OP forecast, pricing in an optimistic recovery. Stock then retreated because the full-year actual delivered a net income decline via the impairment surprise, and FY2027 guidance points to profits declining again. Thinly traded micro-cap amplifies sentiment swings. No broker coverage found.
▸ Seafood majors — aquaculture turnaround + record earnings 2 names
Nissui and Maruha Nichiro both delivered post-merger or all-time-record operating profits driven by domestic aquaculture turning profitable (yellowtail/mackerel prices up + survival rates better), strong overseas expansion (Chilean salmon, North American processing), and large dividend hikes. Both stocks re-rated as the market recognized durable earnings improvement.
1332 Nissui ▸
Revenue: ↑ +5.1% Op profit: ↑ +27.2% Net profit: ↑ +8.4% Stock: +~55% over past year (52wk low ¥830 → current ~¥1,257; high ¥1,570)
分類: 水産・食品 / Seafood & Food Processing
Genuine research (combined-prompt verification) 📚 FY2026 revenue ¥931.3B + OP ¥40.4B confirmed across stockanalysis.com, irbank.net, and diamond.jp/zaikei. Net profit ¥27…
Company overview: Nissui (formerly Nippon Suisan, founded 1911, TSE Prime) is Japan's leading integrated seafood company — it fishes, farms, processes, and sells marine products across 26 countries. Four segments: Marine Products (fishing + aquaculture in Japan and Chile), Food Products (domestic ready-meals + the US Gorton's brand), Fine Business (pharmaceutical-grade EPA/DHA from fish), and Logistics. Think of it as turning the entire ocean into a supply chain. How did business move? FY2026 (year ended March 2026): Revenue ¥931.3 billion (+5.1% YoY), Operating profit ¥40.4 billion (+27.2% — first time ever above ¥40 billion, a record), Net profit ¥27.5 billion (+8.4%). Dividend raised to ¥32/share from ¥28 — the fifth consecutive annual increase. Buyback of ¥6 billion; total shareholder returns reached 57% of profit. For FY2027 the company guides ¥980B revenue (+5%) and ¥42.5B operating profit (+5%), another record target. Why did business move this way? (1) Domestic aquaculture turned around dramatically because yellowtail and mackerel prices rose while improved farming survival rates cut costs — a previously loss-making business flipped to a major profit engine. (2) South American salmon scaled up; Chilean output grew, and Nissui completed a full buyout of PESQUERA YADRAN in January 2026, adding Atlantic salmon to its existing trout and coho lineup. (3) North American processing recovered as surimi and fillet prices rose, lifting Gorton's-related factory utilization. (4) Domestic chilled foods stayed robust on continued convenience-store demand. Why did the stock move this way? 52-week range ~¥830–¥1,570. (1) Sharp drop to ¥795 in April 2025 — Trump tariff panic swept up Japanese equities. (2) Recovery to a then-record ¥1,258 by November 2025 — quarterly results confirmed the aquaculture turnaround. (3) Sudden 5.7% drop in late November 2025 — Chilean acquisition announcement spooked short-term holders on integration risk. (4) Re-ascent into 2026 — Q3 results showed operating profit +26.5% YoY, the company raised guidance again, dividend went to ¥32, and a major brokerage raised its target price to ¥1,950.
1333 Maruha Nichiro (→ Umios 2026) ▸
Revenue: ↑ +4.7% Op profit: ↑ +14.5% Net profit: ↑ +11.6% Stock: +22–24% YoY (Sep 2025 reference, pre-split basis)
分類: 水産・食品総合 / Seafood & Food Conglomerate
Genuine research (combined-prompt verification) 📚 FY2025 revenue ¥1,078,631M / OP ¥30,381M / NP ¥23,264M confirmed across irbank.net + official IR PDF + NNA/Nikkei. Divid…
Company overview: Maruha Nichiro is Japan's largest integrated seafood and food company. It spans fishing and aquaculture, seafood wholesale trading, processed foods (frozen food, canned fish, fish sausages), pet food manufacturing (from a Thailand plant mainly for North America), and fine chemicals. Despite the "fishing company" image, the real profit engine is food ingredient distribution, processed foods, and overseas businesses. In March 2026 the company renamed itself "Umios" to signal a pivot toward becoming an ocean-centered food solutions company. How did business move? FY2025 (April 2024–March 2025): Revenue ¥1.079 trillion (+4.7%) — firmly above the ¥1 trillion mark. Operating profit ¥30.4 billion (+14.5%) — first time exceeding ¥30 billion since the 2007 Maruha-Nichiro merger. Net profit ¥23.3 billion (+11.6%). Dividend raised from ¥85 to ¥110 (+29% — fifth straight year of increases). For FY2026, management guides operating profit ¥27 billion (-11.1%), reflecting ¥5 billion in one-off transformation costs (name change, office relocation, rebranding); underlying ~¥32 billion (+5%). H1 FY2026 OP +16.6% YoY, tracking ahead. 1-for-3 stock split executed December 2025. Why did business move this way? (1) Pet food/processed foods boom — Thailand plant exports to North America surged on strong demand. (2) Seafood trading efficiency — inventory management discipline turned the unit profitable. (3) Overseas leap — revenue +17.7% to ¥251B, operating profit +77.3% to ¥14B, driven by European salmon market prices. (4) Recovery in tuna and scallop markets — North American and European buyers absorbed volumes diverted from China. Why did the stock move this way? Stock rose ~22–24% over the past year (¥3,400–3,700 range late 2025). (1) Record earnings re-rating: hitting OP above ¥30B post-merger prompted institutional re-evaluation of a stock long below book value (PBR ~0.8x). (2) Dividend jump pushed yield above 3%. (3) November 2025 stock split + shareholder gift announcement drove a year-high. (4) The medium-term plan and "Umios" rebrand added a growth narrative. (5) August 2024 institutional share sale of ~¥11.9B caused a temporary 7% drop, recovered as earnings proved resilient.
▸ Seed & agricultural-input recovery 1 names
Kaneko Seeds and Sakata Seed both posted record operating profits as agricultural materials demand normalized post-COVID, premium-variety mix improved margins, and (for Sakata) overseas vegetable seed sales benefited from climate change driving farmer demand for heat-resistant and disease-resistant varieties. Both have low PBR; one (Sakata) executed a self-tender offer to absorb a major shareholder's sale.
1377 Sakata Seed ▸
Revenue: ↑ +4.8% Op profit: ↑ +16.8% Net profit: ↓ -39.9% (FY2024 real-estate gain non-recurrence) Stock: Broadly flat to modest gain; 52-week range ¥3,310–¥4,630, current ~¥4,160 (approx. +5–10% YoY)
分類: 種苗メーカー / Seed & Agri-Inputs
Genuine research (combined-prompt verification) 📚 Revenue ¥92.9B + OP ¥12.3B confirmed across note.com/tousi525 and stockanalysis.com. NP -39.9% confirmed both; reason (F…
Company overview: Sakata Seed is a Yokohama-based seed company that develops, produces, and sells vegetable seeds, flower seeds, bulbs, seedlings, and agricultural/horticultural supplies. It is Japan's largest seed company and roughly the 6th largest in the world, selling to over 170 countries. Around 80% of revenue comes from overseas. Holds ~65% of the global broccoli seed market and ~70% of the torenia flower seed market. Aggressive M&A expansion since 2013 (including Sana Seeds in the Netherlands and Isla Sementes in Brazil in 2023). Fiscal year ends May 31. How did business move? FY2025 (ended May 2025): record revenue ¥92.9B (+4.8%), record operating profit ¥12.3B (+16.8%), ordinary profit ¥12.3B (+10.7%). Net profit fell sharply to ¥9.7B (−39.9%) — but this reflects non-recurrence of a FY2024 real estate sale gain, not core business weakness. Dividend raised to ¥75 (+¥10 — five consecutive increases). For FY2026 the company guided conservatively (¥95.5B revenue, ¥11B OP). Actual Q1 FY2026 came in at OP +63.5% and net profit +222%, raising strong upward-revision expectations. Why did business move this way? Three main drivers: (1) Overseas vegetable seed strength — broccoli, pumpkin, tomato, watermelon, and sunflower performed well across major regions because Sakata's heat-tolerant and disease-resistant varieties are increasingly valuable as climate conditions worsen. (2) Weak yen added revenue through favorable currency translation. (3) Gross margin improvement through mix shift to premium varieties and successful price pass-through. Net profit fell only because FY2024 included a non-recurring real estate gain. Why did the stock move this way? Stock traded ¥3,310–¥4,630 over the year, ending around ¥4,160 — broadly flat to modestly higher. (1) October 2025 surge: Sakata announced blowout Q1 results alongside a self-tender offer (TOB) for its own shares — the chairman's asset-management company wanted to sell ~1M shares; instead of letting them hit the open market, Sakata bought them via TOB, absorbing supply. (2) Nomura raised target from ¥5,300 → ¥5,700 → ¥6,200 over 2025, signaling growing analyst conviction. At PBR ~0.9x the stock trades below book value despite strong fundamentals.
▸ Domestic food-shortage beneficiaries 1 names
Hokuto (mushrooms) and Axyz (poultry) both benefited from supply shortfalls elsewhere in the food chain that pushed domestic prices higher. For Hokuto, 2024 vegetable shortages drove shoppers toward stable factory-grown mushrooms; for Axyz, avian flu disruptions abroad raised domestic chicken prices while feed costs fell. Both saw sharp profit jumps, though Hokuto's market viewed FY2025 as one-off while Axyz's stock rose 57% on durable margin expansion.
1381 Axyz ▸
Revenue: ↑ +2.3% Op profit: ↑ +35.1% Net profit: ↑ +38.8% Stock: +57% over 2025 calendar year (¥2,643 end-2024 → ¥4,145 end-2025; currently ~¥3,770 Jun 2026)
分類: 食鳥処理・鶏肉加工 / Poultry Processing & Chicken Products
Genuine research (combined-prompt verification) 📚 Code 1381 confirmed as Axyz. All yen in 億円 (100M yen). FY2025 (Rev ¥264.26億 +2.3% / OP ¥21.21億 +35.1% / NP ¥17.20億 +38.8…
Company overview: Axyz Co., Ltd. (TSE Standard, 1381) is a vertically integrated poultry company headquartered in Kagoshima. It controls the entire chicken supply chain in-house — from feed manufacturing and breeding through to processing and selling finished products like karaage fried chicken and nuggets. About 84% of revenue comes from the food business (processed chicken products), 14% from KFC and Pizza Hut franchise restaurants, and 2% from a renewable-energy operation. Top customers FoodLink (21%) and Nicherei Fresh (18%) account for ~39% of sales combined. Market cap ~¥21.2 billion (small-cap). How did business move? FY2025 (July 2024 – June 2025): Revenue ¥26.4B (+2.3%), Operating profit ¥2.12B (+35.1%), Net profit ¥1.72B (+38.8%). FY2026 9-month YTD (to March 2026): Operating profit reached ¥2.9B, more than double the same period a year ago (+122%). The company twice revised full-year FY2026 forecast upward; latest guidance calls for revenue ¥28.8B (+9%), OP ¥3.4B (+60%), NP ¥2.4B (+40%), dividend ¥112.5. Why did business move this way? Three things happened at once. (1) Chicken prices rose sharply — avian influenza outbreaks in 2024–25 disrupted imported chicken supply (Brazil supplies ~70% of Japan's imports), so domestic market prices climbed, and Axyz — as a domestic producer — sold at those higher prices. (2) Feed costs fell — corn and soybean meal prices came down in 2025, cutting Axyz's biggest input cost and dramatically widening margins. (3) KFC store expansion added revenue from new outlets. Why did the stock move this way? The stock rose ~57% during calendar 2025 (¥2,643 → ¥4,145), peaking at ¥4,600 in January 2026, before pulling back to ~¥3,770 as of June 2026. (1) Successive upward profit revisions — costs dropped and chicken prices rose simultaneously, drawing in investors as the company kept upgrading earnings forecasts. (2) Thematic buying around the avian flu story — domestic chicken producers attracted attention as poultry supply disruption concerns spread. (3) Cheap valuation unwinding — Axyz was trading below book value (PBR <1x) and at low P/E; once improved earnings became clear the stock re-rated upward. The 2026 pullback reflects uncertainty about whether the favorable chicken market will hold.

How we built this view

For each company, an AI model (Claude Sonnet 4.6) is given one carefully-worded prompt and access to the web. It searches Japanese sources, reads the most relevant pages, drafts a plain-language summary in Japanese, then runs a self-verification pass against a second source before producing the final result. Every URL it used is recorded; every number it claims is labeled with its fiscal-year scope; anything it could not confirm is honestly flagged. The whole pipeline is designed so that a reviewer can audit any output by reading the notes field and following the source URLs.

📊 Pipeline at a glance 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  1. INPUT                                                       │
│  ────────                                                       │
│  • Stock code (e.g. 9201)                                       │
│  • Locked prompt template (see "Input prompt" below)            │
└──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┘
                           │
                           ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  2. MIDDLE STEPS  (~6 minutes per company, runs unattended)     │
│  ─────────────                                                  │
│  a) Claude Sonnet 4.6 reads the prompt                          │
│  b) Searches the web 5–10 times (Japanese sources)              │
│  c) Reads 3–5 pages in detail (IR, Nikkei, kabutan, irbank…)    │
│  d) Drafts the 4-section summary in plain Japanese              │
│  e) VERIFICATION PASS — checks every headline number against    │
│     a second independent source. Disagreements logged in notes. │
└──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┘
                           │
                           ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  3. OUTPUT                                                      │
│  ─────────                                                      │
│  • jp_summary           — 4 sections, plain Japanese            │
│  • rev_dir / op_dir /                                           │
│    net_dir              — "up" / "down" / "flat" tags           │
│  • stock_yoy_estimate   — e.g. "-20% from peak"                 │
│  • biz_classification   — e.g. "フルサービス大手 / Full-service │
│                                  major"                         │
│  • sources              — every URL the model actually used     │
│  • notes                — verification audit trail              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📝 The input prompt (locked, identical for every company)

This is the EXACT text given to the model. {code} and {company_name} are the only things that change per company — everything else is byte-identical across every run. 

You're helping someone quickly understand a Japanese listed company and why
its business and stock have moved. Write the way you'd explain it to a
smart colleague who has never heard of this company and is reading this
once — plain, clear, easy to get on the first read. No finance jargon.
Whenever you give a number, add a few words on what it means (big or
small? good, bad, or normal?). Keep each section to a short paragraph
or two — tight and readable, not a wall of text.

Research stock code {code} ({company_name}) using web search.

For the NUMBERS, use the most recent report. But for the REASONS, look
wider — the real cause is often a trend or chain of events over the
past several quarters or the past year.

Searching tip — useful Japanese terms when hunting for catalysts:
レーティング / 格上げ / 格下げ / 目標株価 / TOB / M&A /
業績予想修正 / 上方修正 / 下方修正 / 適時開示

Cover these four sections, in plain Japanese. Each section MUST start with its
header wrapped in **bold:** markdown so the renderer displays it as bold —
write the headers EXACTLY like this:

  **会社概要:** [What the company actually does.]
  **業績の動き:** [Latest results and guidance, numbers explained plainly.]
  **業績が動いた理由:** [WHY business moved that way.]
  **株価が動いた理由:** [WHY the stock moved over the past year.]

(Do NOT use 【会社概要】 brackets, ALL-CAPS headers, or plain headers without
**...**  — only `**Header:**` will render as bold in the HTML view.)

EXPLAIN, don't just NAME: after each reason, add a short "because…"
linking cause to effect in plain everyday terms.

Two limits, so "explaining" never becomes "making things up":
- General common-sense logic is fine (how tariffs work, what PBR<1
  means, etc.) — that's general knowledge.
- Do NOT invent company-specific facts or motives unless a source
  says so. If you don't know the mechanism, explain only the general
  logic and stop.

VERIFICATION PASS — MANDATORY BEFORE OUTPUT:
1. Re-read each headline number against the source you cited.
2. Pull a SECOND source for the most consequential numbers (latest FY
   actuals, dividend, current stock price). If they MATCH, use the
   number. If they DISAGREE, write BOTH in notes and use the more
   authoritative source (IR > Nikkei > Yahoo Finance > aggregator).
3. Disambiguate measures with multiple valid definitions (e.g.
   passenger count: total / domestic / international). State which.
4. Flag any specific claim (named broker action, named M&A, dated
   event) you can't point to a source for. Either remove or move to
   notes as "unverified."
5. Label each headline number in notes with fiscal-year scope:
   "Revenue ¥X — FY2026 actual, source: [URL]" etc.

Two rules: Use the web for everything. Only give a reason if a source
actually says it. If you can't find why, say so plainly — never invent.

ALSO output these compact tags (used for the card display in the UI):
- rev_dir:             "up" | "down" | "flat"   — revenue direction YoY
- op_dir:              "up" | "down" | "flat"   — operating profit direction YoY
- net_dir:             "up" | "down" | "flat"   — net profit direction YoY
- stock_yoy_estimate:  short string like "-20% from peak" or "+5% YoY"
                       — direction & rough magnitude of the past-12-month stock move
- biz_classification:  short bilingual label like
                       "フルサービス大手 / Full-service major"
                       — what kind of business this is, in one phrase

Output strict JSON:
{ jp_summary, rev_dir, op_dir, net_dir, stock_yoy_estimate,
  biz_classification, sources, notes }
(Japanese summary only — no English summary is produced.)
あなたは、日本の上場企業について、その事業と株価がなぜ動いたかを誰かに
素早く理解してもらう手助けをしています。この企業を初めて聞く頭の良い同
僚に、一度読むだけで分かるように説明する書き方をしてください — 平易で明
瞭、初見で頭に入る文章にすること。金融専門用語は使わない。数字を出すた
びに、それが何を意味するかを短く添えること(大きいか小さいか? 良いか悪
いか普通か?)。各セクションは短い段落1〜2つ — 読みやすくコンパクトに、
文章の壁にならないように。

証券コード {code}({company_name})をWeb検索でリサーチしてください。

数値は最新の決算レポートを使用すること。ただし「理由」についてはもっと
広く見ること — 真の原因は過去数四半期や過去1年にわたるトレンドや出来事
の連鎖にあることが多いから。

検索ヒント — 触媒(カタリスト)を探す際に有用な日本語キーワード:
レーティング / 格上げ / 格下げ / 目標株価 / TOB / M&A /
業績予想修正 / 上方修正 / 下方修正 / 適時開示

以下の4つのセクションを、平易な日本語でカバーしてください。各セクション
は必ず **bold:** マークダウンでヘッダーをラップすること(レンダラーが太字
として表示するため) — ヘッダーは正確に下記の形式で記述すること:

  **会社概要:** [この会社が実際に何をしているか]
  **業績の動き:** [直近の決算と現在のガイダンス、数字を平易に説明]
  **業績が動いた理由:** [事業がなぜそう動いたか]
  **株価が動いた理由:** [株価が過去1年でなぜ動いたか]

(【会社概要】の角括弧、ALL-CAPS、**...**なしの平文ヘッダーは使用しない
こと — `**Header:**` 形式のみがHTMLビューで太字レンダリングされます。)

「説明する」のであって「名前を挙げる」だけではない: 各理由の後に、原因
と結果を結ぶ「なぜなら…」を1〜2文で短く付け加えること。

「説明」が「捏造」にならないための2つの制限:
- 一般的・常識的なロジック(関税の仕組み、PBR<1の意味など)はOK —
  これは一般知識であり、企業固有の主張ではない。
- ソースが明示していない限り、企業固有の事実や動機を捏造しないこと。
  メカニズムが分からない場合は、一般ロジックのみ説明して止めること。

検証パス(MANDATORY、出力前に必ず実施):
1. 述べる各主要数値を、引用ソースに対して再読確認すること。
2. 最も重要な数値(直近FY実績、配当、現在の株価)について、2つ目の
   独立ソースを引いて確認すること。一致すればその数値を使用; 不一致
   なら両方の数値をnotesに記入し、より権威のあるソース
   (IR > 日経 > Yahoo Finance > アグリゲーター)を本文で使用すること。
3. 複数の有効な定義がある指標を明確化(例: 旅客数 — 全体/国内/国際の
   どれか)。どれを指しているか明示すること。
4. 特定の主張(名指しの証券会社アクション、名指しのM&A、日付付き
   イベント)で、ソースを指せないものはnotesに「unverified」として
   移動するか削除すること。
5. 各主要数値をnotesで会計年度スコープと共にラベル付け:
   「売上 ¥X — FY2026実績、ソース: [URL]」など。

2つの重要なルール: 何でもWebで調べること。ソースが実際に言っている
理由のみを述べること。もしなぜか分からない場合は、平易にそう言うこと
— 決して捏造しないこと。

以下のコンパクトタグも出力すること(UIのカード表示で使用):
- rev_dir:             "up" | "down" | "flat"   — 売上の前年同期比方向
- op_dir:              "up" | "down" | "flat"   — 営業利益の前年同期比方向
- net_dir:             "up" | "down" | "flat"   — 純利益の前年同期比方向
- stock_yoy_estimate:  「-20% from peak」「+5% YoY」のような短い文字列
                       — 過去12か月の株価動向の方向と概略の大きさ
- biz_classification:  「フルサービス大手 / Full-service major」のような
                       短い日英ラベル — どのような事業かを一言で

厳密JSONで出力:
{ jp_summary, rev_dir, op_dir, net_dir, stock_yoy_estimate,
  biz_classification, sources, notes }
(日本語要約のみ — 英語要約は生成しません)
🔍 What happens in the middle (the AI's research process)

The AI doesn't just answer from memory — it actively researches each company on the live web. A typical run looks like this:

  1. Search the web (5–10 times) — primarily Japanese sources. Targets include kabutan, Nikkei, Yahoo Finance Japan, irbank, IR pages, traders.co.jp, gamebiz.
  2. Open and read 3–5 pages in detail — pulling specific numbers (revenue, profit, dividend, stock price) and event details.
  3. Draft the summary in Japanese — 4 sections in plain language. Every reason gets a "because…" clause.
  4. Verification pass — for each headline number, pull a SECOND source and check it matches. If sources disagree, BOTH numbers go in notes and the more authoritative one is used in the prose.
  5. Output as strict JSON — jp_summary (the prose) + 5 compact card tags + sources + notes.

Wall-clock time: about 5–7 minutes per company, fully unattended. ~42,000 tokens of context per run on average (Japanese-only output).

📦 The output format

Every company has this exact JSON shape, stored per-sector: 

{
  "9201": {
    "name":               "Japan Airlines",
    "jp_summary":         "[4 sections in plain Japanese, ~1,500–2,500 chars]",
    "rev_dir":            "up",
    "op_dir":             "up",
    "net_dir":            "up",
    "stock_yoy_estimate": "-20% from peak",
    "biz_classification": "フルサービス大手 / Full-service major",
    "sources":            ["url1", "url2", ...],
    "notes":              "[verification audit trail + anything unconfirmed]"
  }
}

Why the notes field matters: this is the audit trail. Reviewer reads notes first.

⚖️ Quality controls
  • Anti-fabrication rule — the prompt explicitly bans naming specific events unless a source backs them.
  • Two-source verification — headline numbers checked against a SECOND independent source.
  • Fiscal-year disambiguation — notes label whether each number is FY-actual, FY-guidance, or prior-year.
  • Measure disambiguation — multi-definition metrics get an explicit which-one-we-used statement.
  • Calibration testing — pipeline validated on companies with KNOWN hidden catalysts. Sonnet caught all 9 of 9 in fresh runs.
💡 Worked example — Japan Airlines (9201)

Input given to the model: the locked prompt above, with {code}=9201 and {company_name}=Japan Airlines / JAL / 日本航空.

What the model did (middle steps):

  • Searched ~7 queries in JP and EN
  • Fetched 6 pages in detail
  • Drafted the Japanese summary
  • Verification pass caught a discrepancy and resolved it

Output produced: ~1,700-char Japanese summary + 10 source URLs + 1,200-char verification audit trail. Wall-clock: 5.5 min. Batched cost: ~$0.17.

💰 Cost & scale
  • Per company: ~$0.17 batched (~$0.27 real-time)
  • Cost breakdown per company (batched): input tokens ~$0.06 + output tokens ~$0.045 + web search fees ~$0.065 = ~$0.17
  • For the full ~2,900-company R+ universe: ~$490 total via Anthropic Batch API (Japanese-only output)
  • Turnaround: Batch API within 24h. Real-time API ~6 min/company.
